nativeRange.habitat: Densely vegetated lowland marshes, shallow floodplain rice fields, and slow-moving forest streams with soft, tannin-stained blackwater, leaf-litter substrate, and minimal current; Mae Khlong basin near Ratchaburi and Nakhon Nayok Province, central Thailand

decorPreferences: dense planting with broad-leaved species (Cryptocoryne, Microsorium)
floating plants to diffuse surface light
dark substrate (fine sand or fine gravel)
leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) to release tannins
driftwood for additional cover and tannin release

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, microcrustaceans, and insect larvae. In the aquarium, offer small live and frozen foods (microworm, baby brine shrimp, Daphnia, bloodworm) as the dietary staple. Will also accept high-quality micro-pellets and fine flake as supplement. Food size must be appropriate for the tiny mouth — standard flake should be crushed before feeding.

goodWith: other small peaceful rasboras (Boraras, Trigonostigma espei)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) in species-focused setups
small peaceful nano fish of similar size
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them (virtually all standard community fish)
boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Trigonostigma somphongsi; verify availability and licensing before use.

summary: Somphong’s Rasbora is one of the world’s tiniest cyprinids at only 2–3 cm, endemic to a handful of floodplain marshes in central Thailand and IUCN Critically Endangered. It demands soft, acidic blackwater and a species-focused nano setup, making it a rewarding but specialist fish.
careNotes:
Somphong’s Rasbora (Trigonostigma somphongsi) is among the smallest and rarest rasboras in the hobby. Adults reach only 25–30 mm SL — noticeably smaller than the Lambchop Rasbora (T. espei) and significantly smaller than the Harlequin Rasbora (T. heteromorpha). The species is IUCN Critically Endangered, restricted to a narrow range of lowland marshes and seasonal floodplains in central Thailand (Mae Khlong basin, Nakhon Nayok Province); it was presumed extinct in the wild for roughly 30 years before a trio appeared in a German aquarium-trade shipment in 2006. Because wild populations are critically small, captive breeding efforts by specialist hobbyists carry genuine conservation value.

Water chemistry is non-negotiable: soft (gH 1–8), acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), warm (22–26°C) blackwater conditions are required for long-term health. Reverse osmosis or similarly soft source water is strongly recommended; hard tap water causes gradual decline. Add Indian almond leaves or alder cones to release tannins and further acidify the water. Peat filtration is an acceptable alternative. Dim lighting created by a heavy canopy of floating plants is important — the species is uncomfortable under bright illumination and will show best colour under subdued conditions. A 60 × 30 cm footprint (approximately 40 L) is a workable minimum for a group, though larger is better.

Keep in schools of 10 or more; smaller groups produce nervous, washed-out fish. Due to the species’ tiny size, suitable tankmates are extremely limited — species-only setups or pairings with dwarf shrimp are generally safest. Diet must consist primarily of micro-sized live or frozen foods (baby brine shrimp, microworm, Daphnia, bloodworm); standard flake must be crushed to powder. Intermediate care rating reflects strict water-chemistry and feeding requirements rather than aggression or disease fragility.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer that attaches eggs to the underside of broad plant leaves (Cryptocoryne, Microsorium) or deposits them among fine-leaved moss. Use a dedicated breeding tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.0, near-zero hardness), dim lighting, and broad-leaved plants or java moss. A group spawn with multiple males and females is recommended. Eggs hatch in 24–48 hours; fry become free-swimming within approximately one week. First foods must be infusoria or Paramecium, progressing to Artemia nauplii as the fry grow. Parental care is absent — adults will consume eggs if not separated or if spawning mops/plants are moved to a separate rearing container. Breeding in captivity has been achieved but is considered moderately difficult due to water-chemistry precision requirements and the need for extremely fine first foods.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Trigonostigma somphongsi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (25–30 mm SL), temperature 22–26°C, pH 5.0–7.0, hardness 18–143 ppm, tank minimum 60 × 30 cm, diet (micropredator; live/frozen micro-foods), breeding method (eggs on broad leaves, 24–48 h incubation), native range (Mae Klong basin, western Thailand), habitat (soft acidic blackwater forest streams), and conservation context.

additional.name: FishBase — Trigonostigma somphongsi
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.0–6.5), native range (Mae Khlong basin, Thailand), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes, Rasborinae), and IUCN CR status. Note: FishBase lists max length 10.5 cm TL which is widely regarded as a data error; SeriouslyFish 25–30 mm SL used instead, consistent with all hobbyist and field sources.

additional.name: IUCN — Saving Somphong’s Rasbora: A Critically Endangered Fish in Thailand
additional.notes: Conservation context: IUCN CR status confirmed, native range described as Mae Khlong basin near Ratchaburi to outskirts of Bangkok; habitat described as densely vegetated lowland freshwater marshes dependent on seasonal flooding; species identified in Thung Pak Phi, Nakhon Nayok Province in 2023 surveys. Confirms species was presumed extinct for over 30 years.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Trigonostigma somphongsi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Danionidae, Rasborinae, Cypriniformes), native range (lower Mae Klong Basin, Ratchaburi Province), habitat description (deeply vegetated tannin-stained waters), conservation status (Critically Endangered, IUCN 3.1), and the 2006 rediscovery event (trio found in German trade shipment among Boraras urophthalmoides).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op Forum — Somphong’s Rasbora spawning behavior
additional.notes: Hobbyist breeding discussion confirming egg-laying on plant leaves and soft-acidic water requirements for spawning. Corroborates SeriouslyFish breeding notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Deposits eggs on undersides of broad plant leaves or among moss; requires very soft acidic water (pH 5.5–6.0, near-zero hardness) and dim lighting. Fry demand infusoria or Paramecium as first food. Adults show no parental care and will eat eggs.
